document: Horizontal port scan. Multiple flows from the same source to many different destination IPs, all targeting the same destination port. Each flow typically shows conn_state S0 (SYN with no reply) or REJ (RST reply), a single packet sent, zero application bytes, and near-zero duration. A single such flow shows: one SYN packet sent, no reply, no data. This pattern requires observing many flows to confirm. A single flow with these characteristics — one unanswered SYN, no data — is consistent with being part of a network-wide port sweep (reconnaissance scanning one port across a subnet), but a single flow alone could equally be a connection to an unreachable host or a firewall-dropped SYN. Confidence increases with the number of similar flows from the same source in a short time window. Note: this is a multi-flow pattern; confirming it requires observing multiple related flows, not just this single connection.

Vertical port scan (service enumeration). Multiple flows from the same source to the same destination IP, each targeting a different destination port. Individual flows show conn_state S0 or REJ, minimal packets, zero or near-zero bytes, and short duration. This pattern requires observing many flows to confirm. A single flow with S0 or REJ to one port is ambiguous on its own; it becomes indicative of service enumeration only when accompanied by similar flows to other ports on the same host. The goal of the attacker is typically to discover which services are running. Note: this is a multi-flow pattern; confirming it requires observing multiple related flows, not just this single connection.

Brute-force or credential-spraying activity. Many short-lived flows in rapid succession to the same destination port, often an authentication service. Individual flows are brief (sub-second to a few seconds), carry small payload, and show conn_state SF (completed but brief), REJ, RSTO, or RSTR in varying mix. Each flow resembles a quick login attempt. This pattern requires observing many flows to confirm. A single short connection to an authentication port is ordinary; it becomes suspicious when dozens or hundreds of similar flows appear in a short window from the same source — consistent with automated credential guessing. A mix of SF (successful auth?) among mostly REJ/RSTO/RSTR (failures) can indicate a successful compromise after many attempts. Note: this is a multi-flow pattern; confirming it requires observing multiple related flows, not just this single connection.

Beaconing / command-and-control periodic check-in. Flows from the same source to the same destination IP and port, repeating at roughly regular intervals (seconds to minutes). Each flow is short, carries small and relatively uniform payload (similar byte counts each time), and completes normally (conn_state SF). This pattern requires observing many flows over time to confirm periodicity. A single short SF flow with small payload is entirely normal; it becomes suspicious only when the same source-destination pair shows a repeating cadence. Consistent with malware or implant checking in with a command-and-control server at fixed intervals, but also consistent with legitimate polling, health-checks, or keep-alive mechanisms. Note: this is a multi-flow pattern; confirming it requires observing multiple related flows, not just this single connection.

Fan-out communication (one source to many destinations). A single source IP communicating with a large number of distinct destination IPs within a short time window. Individual flows may vary in port, state, and volume. This pattern requires observing many flows to confirm. A single flow to one destination is unremarkable; the pattern emerges only at scale. Fan-out is consistent with worm propagation (scanning then exploiting), DDoS participation (attacking many targets), spam distribution, or legitimate CDN/API client behavior. Context — the ports targeted, the conn_states, and the payload — distinguishes malicious from benign. Note: this is a multi-flow pattern; confirming it requires observing multiple related flows, not just this single connection.

Fan-in communication (many sources to one destination). Many distinct source IPs connecting to the same destination IP and port within a short time window. Individual flows may be short or carry varying payload. This pattern requires observing many flows to confirm. Fan-in to a single host is consistent with DDoS flooding, botnet command-and-control (many bots reporting to one C2 server), or legitimate high-traffic services (popular web server, API endpoint). The distinction depends on whether the destination is a known service and whether the traffic volume and source diversity are expected. Note: this is a multi-flow pattern; confirming it requires observing multiple related flows, not just this single connection.

Client-dominant bulk transfer. A single flow where the originator (client) sent substantially more bytes than the responder — byte ratio heavily favoring the client side (e.g. orig_bytes several times larger than resp_bytes, commonly ratios above roughly 5:1 client-to-server). Total volume is significant (thousands of bytes or more). Connection typically completes (SF) or is reset after the transfer (RSTO). Observable from a single flow. A heavily client-dominant transfer may indicate data upload, file exfiltration, large POST request, backup/sync operation, or C2 data staging. Whether it is malicious depends on the destination, the service, and whether such outbound volume is expected. The byte asymmetry alone does not prove exfiltration — legitimate uploads produce the same signature. Note: this pattern can be assessed from a single flow record.

Server-dominant bulk transfer. A single flow where the responder (server) sent substantially more bytes than the originator — byte ratio heavily favoring the server side (e.g. resp_bytes many times larger than orig_bytes, commonly ratios above roughly 5:1 server-to-client). Total volume is significant. Connection typically completes normally (SF). Observable from a single flow. A heavily server-dominant transfer may indicate file download, web resource retrieval, database query result, software update, or data extraction from a compromised server. Benign when the destination and service are expected; suspicious when the server is unusual, the volume is disproportionate, or the service does not normally produce large responses. Note: this pattern can be assessed from a single flow record.

Reset after data exchange. A single flow where application data was exchanged (orig_bytes and/or resp_bytes greater than zero) but the connection ended with a RST rather than a normal FIN close — conn_state RSTO (client reset) or RSTR (server reset). Observable from a single flow. Data followed by an abrupt reset may indicate a C2 check-in followed by cleanup (send command, receive response, reset to avoid lingering state), exploit delivery then teardown, application-level rejection after partial exchange, or simply a client/server error. The combination of non-trivial payload plus reset is more noteworthy than either signal alone. Note: this pattern can be assessed from a single flow record.

Zero-payload established connection. A single flow where the TCP handshake completed and the connection closed normally (conn_state SF) but no application data was transferred — both orig_bytes and resp_bytes are zero. Observable from a single flow. A fully established connection with zero payload may indicate TLS or protocol-capability probing (testing whether a port accepts connections), credential testing that fails at the application handshake stage, a keepalive-only or health-check connection, or a monitoring probe. Whether it is suspicious depends on the destination port and whether such empty connections are expected for that service. Note: this pattern can be assessed from a single flow record.

Retransmission-heavy flow. A single flow whose TCP history contains a high proportion of retransmission flags ('T' for client retransmissions, 't' for server retransmissions) relative to normal data and control packets — for example, retransmissions making up a third or more of the total packet exchange. Observable from a single flow. Heavy retransmission may indicate network congestion or packet loss on the path, an unreliable or overloaded endpoint, or deliberate evasion techniques (retransmitting segments with different payloads to confuse IDS TCP reassembly). Whether it is benign (poor network quality) or malicious (evasion) depends on context — if only certain flows to a specific destination show high retransmission while others do not, evasion is more likely. Note: this pattern can be assessed from a single flow record.

Long-duration low-volume persistent connection. A single flow with an unusually long duration (minutes to hours) but low byte and packet volume relative to the time span — for example, a few hundred bytes over several minutes. Connection may still be open (S1) or may have closed normally (SF). Observable from a single flow. A persistent connection with minimal data may indicate a backdoor or reverse-shell tunnel held open for on-demand use, an idle VPN or SSH session, a long-polling or WebSocket connection, or a keepalive mechanism. Whether it is suspicious depends on the destination, the port, and whether such long-lived low-traffic sessions are expected in the environment. Note: this pattern can be assessed from a single flow record.
